planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist perlite or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich potting mix in partial to full shade after frost; metallic-leaf begonia is particularly sensitive to waterlogging and cold drafts — use a very freely draining potting mix and avoid temperatures below 55°F, which cause the hairy foliage to develop gray patches and increase susceptibility to Botrytis.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, humus-rich potting mix in partial to full shade; metallic-leaf begonia is a Brazilian shrubby begonia whose primary distinction is the extraordinary, burnished, metallic quality of its foliage — the large, asymmetric, heart-shaped leaves are deep olive-green to rich bronze-green in color but have a pronounced silvery-metallic sheen or gloss over the surface that catches and reflects light in a way that appears genuinely metallic rather than simply shiny, and the leaf surface is marked with prominent, dark-red to deep-purple veining that creates a vivid color contrast against the metallic-green ground. White hairs cover both leaf surfaces. The plant grows with an upright, branching, cane-forming habit and produces loose clusters of pale-pink flowers at the branch tips throughout the warm season. The metallic foliage is the primary ornamental feature and is most vivid in partial-shade conditions where direct light can catch the leaf surface at angles that maximize the metallic reflection. An excellent specimen for shaded tropical patios, conservatories, and zone 10–12 shade gardens.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in moist perlite under intermittent mist, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade after frost; ming aralia is sensitive to cold drafts and temperatures below 55°F, which cause leaf drop — protect from cold air movement and avoid sites exposed to winter winds in zone 10, where the plant is marginally hardy.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; ming aralia (parsley aralia, wild coffee tree) is an Indo-Pacific tropical evergreen shrub in the ginseng family (Araliaceae) that produces some of the most intricately textured and refined tropical foliage available for shaded garden positions — the leaves are compound and deeply divided, with each leaflet further divided into multiple, small, toothed, crinkled, irregular segments that create a dense, feathery, parsley-like to fern-like appearance at close range and an impression of extraordinary, finely subdivided textural richness from a distance. The thin branches grow in a graceful, architectural, often slightly irregular pattern that resembles a natural bonsai. Ming aralia is frequently grown as an indoor specimen and shaped into bonsai form. Outdoors in zone 10–12 it grows into a substantial shrub or small tree to 15 feet with similarly intricate foliage at a much larger scale. The combination of shade tolerance, fine texture, and elegant form makes it one of the most useful and refined tropical foliage shrubs for shaded positions in the tropical garden.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings under mist or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich, freely draining soil in partial to full shade after frost; false aralia is sensitive to overwatering and root rot — use a freely draining potting mix and allow the top inch to dry between waterings, especially in cooler conditions where the plant's water uptake is reduced.

directions: Root semi-hardwood cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; false aralia (spider aralia) is a New Caledonian tropical tree in the ginseng family (Araliaceae, now reclassified to Plerandra elegantissima from Schefflera elegantissima) with one of the most dramatic and phase-changing foliage sequences of any tropical plant — juvenile plants (the form most commonly sold as a houseplant) produce long, narrow, deeply serrated, copper-bronze to dark blackish-brown, almost spider-like leaflets radiating from each leaf stalk like the fingers of a dark, elaborately toothed hand, creating an extraordinarily refined and dramatic appearance quite unlike any common houseplant. As the plant matures and reaches tree size, the leaf form transitions dramatically to large, broad, rounded, glossy-green leaflets similar to a large Schefflera, losing the juvenile spider form. In the landscape it becomes a substantial tree to 20 feet. The juvenile, spider-like foliage form is the primary ornamental feature and is best maintained by keeping the plant in a container where root restriction prevents rapid maturation. An exceptional architectural accent for shaded tropical patios and zone 10–12 shade gardens.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in moist perlite or water, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich potting mix in partial to full shade after frost; grape ivy is adaptable to a wide range of conditions but requires protection from temperatures below 45°F, which cause leaf drop and defoliation — in zone 9, grow in protected positions against south-facing walls or as a container specimen moved indoors in winter.

directions: Root stem cuttings or plant container specimens in well-drained, humus-rich potting mix in partial to full shade; grape ivy is a Venezuelan tropical evergreen vine of the grape family (Vitaceae) that has been among the most widely grown and reliable shade-tolerant tropical foliage vines for over a century — it climbs by tendrils to form dense, leafy masses of three-parted (trifoliate), broadly rhombic (diamond-shaped, hence rhombifolia), glossy, dark-green leaves with prominent, rusty-brown, finely hairy undersides that give the plant an attractive textured quality when the foliage is disturbed. The lustrous, dark-green foliage, reliable shade tolerance, and easy cultivation make it an enduringly popular choice for covering shaded walls, trellises, and structures in tropical and subtropical gardens, and as a trailing vine in large containers and hanging baskets indoors or in sheltered outdoor positions. 'Ellen Danica' (oak-leaf grape ivy) has more deeply lobed, oak-leaf-like leaflets and is slightly more cold-sensitive. Outdoors in zone 10–12 it can reach 15 feet or more on suitable supports in shade conditions where most other vines would fail to thrive.
sources: Clemson Cooperative Extension Home & Garden Information Center

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring or fall, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average to humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; creeping lilyturf spreads steadily by stolons and should be planted at 12–15 inch spacing for faster cover — it fills in more quickly than clumping liriope (L. muscari) and will knit into a dense, maintenance-free groundcover within 2–3 seasons.

directions: Divide clumps or plant container specimens in well-drained to moist, average soil in partial to full shade; creeping lilyturf is the spreading, stoloniferous counterpart of the common clumping liriope (L. muscari) — it produces the same narrow, dark-green, strap-shaped, grass-like leaves but spreads steadily outward from each plant by underground stolons, gradually filling in bare shade areas between plants and creating a dense, low, weed-suppressing groundcover mat without the need for reseeding or replanting. The foliage is evergreen and tidy year-round, and in summer the plants produce small, slender spikes of tiny, pale-lavender to white flowers that are less showy than the larger flower spikes of L. muscari but are still a pleasant ornamental addition. Creeping lilyturf is the preferred liriope for quickly covering large shaded areas under trees and along shaded paths, where its spreading habit is an asset rather than a problem. It is extremely shade-tolerant, drought-tolerant once established, and adaptable to difficult, dry, root-competitive shade conditions. An outstanding and low-maintenance shade groundcover for zone 7–11.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
